Query: A 13-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with severe knee, hip, and groin pain. The patient has a past medical history notable only for obesity and asthma. His temperature is 98°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 124/65 mmHg, pulse is 128/min, respirations are 14/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Physical exam is notable for an inability of the patient to bear weight on his left leg and limited range of motion of the left hip. Which of the following is the best management for this patient?

Options:
A: Casting and crutches
B: Immobilization of the hip in a Pavlik harness
C: Supportive therapy and observation
D: Surgical pinning of the femoral head

Document [0] (Title: Prescription of Controlled Substances: Benefits and Risks -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Interprofessional Team Case Study) History of present illness: A 70-year-old confused female is brought to the trauma unit after she was found passed out on the bathroom floor near the toilet. Her husband reports a history of morbid obesity, diabetes mellitus, kidney disease, and chronic right hip pain. She has been scheduled for prosthetic hip surgery replacement, which has been delayed to control her weight. She has been prescribed a combination of hydrocodone and acetaminophen to effectively manage pain for ambulation and daily pool exercises. Lately, the pain has been getting worse, and her husband mentions that occasionally she takes more medicine than prescribed. Physical examination: Her vital signs are as follows: temperature 98.7 °F, blood pressure 100/70 mm Hg, heart rate 110 bpm, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, and O 2 saturation 92%. A physical examination reveals a somnolent female who arouses to a painful stimulus. The heart, lung, and extremity exam is normal. There are no focal neurologic deficits.

Document [15] (Title: Septic Arthritis of the Pediatric Hip -- History and Physical) Children who have septic arthritis of the hip usually present with acute onset of hip joint pain. If they walk, they may be limping and resist weight bearing on their affected leg. They are usually febrile. There may be a history of a recent oropharyngeal infection. When the children are in bed, log rolling of the child will produce severe pain in the hip. Any passive motion of the hip joint is very painful.

Document [21] (Title: End-of-Life Evaluation and Management of Pain -- History and Physical) Physical examination includes a head-to-toe assessment for factors that may contribute to pain. Physical signs of pain include facial grimacing, restlessness, tachypnea, and tachycardia. Patients who have been in bed for prolonged periods can have skin breaks or pressure ulcers in the dependent portions of the body, including the back of the head, shoulder blades, back, sacrum, hips, ankles, and heels. Dryness of the eyes can lead to painful keratitis as well as infections. Patients on long-term oxygen therapy may have skin breaks around the nares, dryness, and episodes of epistaxis, which can be distressing. Patients using noninvasive ventilation modes may have pressure ulcers at the nasal bridge and cheeks. In patients who are intubated and mechanically ventilated, pooling secretions and improper oral care can lead to oral ulcers and dental decay. Head positioning and lack of proper support can lead to painful spasms of the neck muscles.

These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2883) Amputation of lower extremity is a broad surgical category that includes specific procedures such as بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation) and بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation). These lower‑extremity amputations are clinically associated with فشل قلبي احتقاني (congestive heart failure), indicating that patients who undergo or require amputation of the lower limb often have, or are at increased risk for, this form of heart failure. Recognizing the link between lower‑extremity amputation and congestive heart failure is important for comprehensive patient assessment, multidisciplinary management, and optimizing outcomes for individuals affected by these interconnected conditions.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2864) Abdominal pain is clinically associated with malignant hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الخبيث), indicating that patients presenting with abdominal discomfort may be experiencing or at risk for this severe form of high blood pressure. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians to evaluate blood pressure status promptly and manage the underlying hypertensive emergency alongside the abdominal symptoms.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2753) Elevated systolic blood pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the same category as several other high‑pressure disorders. Within the vascular domain, أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) represents an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that requires immediate medical intervention. ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) denotes a chronic increase in pressure within the pulmonary arteries that occurs without an identifiable secondary cause. فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) describes a pattern in which only the systolic component of arterial pressure is elevated. Together, Elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, and systolic hypertension are all considered child entities of the broader Vascular classification, highlighting their shared relevance to vascular pathology and the importance of recognizing and managing these distinct forms of high blood pressure in patient care.
